title: Inference Engineering
text:
1 Preface
This book takes a stack-first view. It starts with the physical data center, moves through hardware and model mechanics, then reaches kernels, serving frameworks, advanced architectures, and production operations.

title: Inference Engineering
text:
1.1 Reader
This book is for engineers, platform teams, applied AI builders, and technical leaders who need to operate LLM-backed systems in production.
The reader should be comfortable reading code, system diagrams, benchmark tables, and architecture notes. Prior experience with distributed systems, APIs, or machine learning helps, but the book should make each inference-specific concept explicit.

title: Inference Engineering
text:
1.2 Book Promise
By the end, a reader should be able to:

Compare AI data center design choices across power, cooling, racks, networks, storage, and operations.
Explain how CPU, GPU, accelerator, memory, and network architecture affect inference performance.
Build a metric system for latency, throughput, cost, quality, and reliability.
Understand why attention, KV cache, long context, MoE, and RL-style workloads change serving behavior.
Read kernel and compiler optimization claims without getting lost.
Deploy and benchmark vLLM on an on-demand GPU environment such as Runpod.
Evaluate speculative decoding, disaggregated inference, and specialized systems such as SambaNova, Cerebras, and Groq.
Operate inference systems with evals, observability, reliability, security, and cost controls.

title: Inference Engineering
text:
1.3 How to Read
The chapters are organized in seven layers:

AI data center design.
Silicon, nodes, and hardware architecture.
Model mechanics that matter for inference.
Kernels, compilers, and runtime primitives.
Framework-first implementation.
Advanced inference architectures.
Production inference engineering.

Each chapter should eventually include:

A concrete system, failure mode, benchmark, or decision.
The core mental model.
Implementation notes.
A lab, artifact, or checklist.
What to measure.
What can go wrong.

The goal is a book that progresses cleanly from physical constraints to operational judgment.

title: 2  Executive Map
text:
2.1 The Stack
The book follows one system from the ground up: the data center that powers the cluster, the node that runs the model, the accelerator that executes kernels, the runtime that schedules tokens, and the product service that turns those tokens into a reliable user experience.
flowchart TB
  A[Power, cooling, and facility constraints]
  B[Racks, clusters, and data center network]
  C[CPU, memory, PCIe, GPU, accelerator, and NIC]
  D[Kernels, compilers, runtimes, and serving engines]
  E[Model mechanics: attention, KV cache, MoE, decoding]
  F[Framework-first implementation: vLLM, Runpod, benchmarks]
  G[Production system: evals, SLOs, observability, cost, reliability]

  A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G

title: 2  Executive Map
text:
2.2 Reader Outcome
The reader should finish with the ability to:

Compare AI data center design choices from cooling to networking.
Explain how hardware architecture shapes inference latency and throughput.
Read model architecture papers through an inference lens.
Deploy and benchmark an LLM server with a framework-first workflow.
Diagnose bottlenecks across CPU, GPU, memory, network, runtime, and model.
Evaluate advanced approaches such as speculative decoding and disaggregation.
Operate inference services with measurable cost, quality, and reliability.

title: 2  Executive Map
text:
2.3 Depth Contract
This is not a chip-design textbook and not a prompt-engineering handbook. The depth target is systems literacy for inference engineers:

Deep where choices change latency, throughput, reliability, or cost.
Practical where the reader needs a runnable workflow.
Comparative where vendors and architectures differ.
Explicit when a topic should be source-verified before publication.

title: 3  AI Data Center Design Space
text:
3.1 Chapter Job
AI inference starts with a physical constraint: every token consumes power, cooling, network capacity, memory bandwidth, accelerator time, and operational attention.
Build the vocabulary required to compare data center choices for inference systems.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
3  AI Data Center Design Space

title: 3  AI Data Center Design Space
text:
3.2 Design Axes








Axis
Design choice
Inference consequence




Facility
Colocation, hyperscale region, managed GPU cloud
Control, speed, procurement risk


Power
Rack density, redundancy, power capping
Maximum accelerator density and burst behavior


Cooling
Air, rear-door heat exchangers, direct liquid, immersion
Rack density, reliability, maintenance model


Cluster
Single node, rack-scale, pod-scale, multi-region
Scheduling, fault domains, data movement


Network
Ethernet, InfiniBand, proprietary fabric
Collective performance and tail latency


Storage
Local NVMe, shared filesystem, object store
Model load time and checkpoint movement


Operations
Bare metal, Kubernetes, managed inference platform
Control surface and team burden
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
3  AI Data Center Design Space

title: 3  AI Data Center Design Space
text:
3.3 Compare/Contrast Pattern
For every data center design choice, ask:

What workload shape does this favor?
What failure mode does it create?
What operational skill does it require?
What measurement proves it works?
What gets harder when the cluster grows?
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
3  AI Data Center Design Space

title: 3  AI Data Center Design Space
text:
3.4 What This Enables Later
The rest of the book repeatedly returns to this chapter. Batching, KV cache, tensor parallelism, disaggregated prefill/decode, and specialized systems only make sense when the physical envelope is visible.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
3  AI Data Center Design Space

title: 4  Power, Cooling, and Rack Density
text:
4.1 Chapter Job
Inference clusters are constrained by heat and power before they are constrained by architectural taste.
Explain how power delivery and cooling choices shape AI hardware density, availability, and operating cost.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
4  Power, Cooling, and Rack Density

title: 4  Power, Cooling, and Rack Density
text:
4.2 Cooling Choices








Cooling model
Why teams choose it
Tradeoff to inspect




Air cooling
Simpler operations and broad facility compatibility
Lower practical rack density


Rear-door heat exchanger
Transitional path for denser racks
Integration and airflow discipline


Direct-to-chip liquid cooling
Higher density and thermal headroom
Facility readiness and maintenance


Immersion cooling
High-density thermal envelope
Ecosystem maturity and service model
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
4  Power, Cooling, and Rack Density

title: 4  Power, Cooling, and Rack Density
text:
4.3 Inference-Specific Questions

Does the workload create steady load or bursty load?
Can the facility support sustained accelerator utilization?
What happens when thermal throttling starts?
Are power caps configured intentionally or discovered during incidents?
Can the same rack support both prefill-heavy and decode-heavy workloads?
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
4  Power, Cooling, and Rack Density

title: 4  Power, Cooling, and Rack Density
text:
4.4 Artifact
Create a rack design card:

Rack density target.
Accelerator count.
Power envelope.
Cooling method.
Network ports per node.
Failure domain.
Measured utilization assumption.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
4  Power, Cooling, and Rack Density

title: 5  AI Networking and Cluster Topology
text:
5.1 Chapter Job
The network decides whether multiple accelerators behave like one system or like several expensive islands.
Compare networking choices for inference: node-local traffic, multi-GPU collectives, multi-node scaling, storage movement, and user-facing serving traffic.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
5  AI Networking and Cluster Topology

title: 5  AI Networking and Cluster Topology
text:
5.2 Traffic Classes

User request traffic.
Model weight and checkpoint movement.
Tensor-parallel and pipeline-parallel communication.
KV cache or activation movement in disaggregated systems.
Observability and control-plane traffic.
Storage reads for model load and warmup.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
5  AI Networking and Cluster Topology

title: 5  AI Networking and Cluster Topology
text:
5.3 Topology Concepts

PCIe root complex.
NUMA locality.
NVLink-class local accelerator fabrics.
Ethernet and InfiniBand fabrics.
Fat-tree, rail-optimized, and pod-level designs.
Oversubscription and failure domains.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
5  AI Networking and Cluster Topology

title: 5  AI Networking and Cluster Topology
text:
5.4 Practical Question
For a given model and target SLO, decide whether the system should scale:

Up inside one node.
Across multiple accelerators in one node.
Across multiple nodes.
Across regions or providers.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design
5  AI Networking and Cluster Topology

title: 6  Data Center Stack Tradeoffs
text:
6.1 Chapter Job
This chapter turns the previous data center topics into decision matrices.
Give the reader a practical method for choosing a stack: cloud GPU rental, managed serverless GPUs, bare metal GPU clusters, specialized inference providers, or fully owned infrastructure.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design

title: 6  Data Center Stack Tradeoffs
text:
6.2 Stack Options








Option
Strength
Cost or risk




Managed API
Fastest product integration
Lowest infrastructure control


GPU cloud instance
Good for experiments and medium control
Availability and price volatility


Managed GPU platform
Faster deployment path
Platform constraints


Bare metal cluster
Maximum control and stable utilization
Procurement and operations burden


Specialized inference system
Strong fit for specific workload classes
Portability and ecosystem risk
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design

title: 6  Data Center Stack Tradeoffs
text:
6.3 Decision Template
Score each option across:

Time to first benchmark.
Time to production.
Hardware control.
Network control.
Runtime control.
Cost predictability.
Failure isolation.
Vendor lock-in.
Team skill required.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design

title: 6  Data Center Stack Tradeoffs
text:
6.4 Bridge
After the data center choices are visible, the book moves inward: what is inside the node, what matters in the accelerator, and which knobs are actually configurable by an inference engineer.
crumbs: Part I: AI Data Center Design

title: 7  Metrics First: The Inference Control Plane
text:
7.1 Chapter Job
Metrics are the control plane for inference engineering. Without them, hardware and runtime discussions collapse into preference.
Define the metrics that let engineers compare hardware, runtimes, model architectures, and deployment designs.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture
7  Metrics First: The Inference Control Plane

title: 7  Metrics First: The Inference Control Plane
text:
7.2 Core Metrics

Time to first token.
Inter-token latency.
Time to last token.
End-to-end product latency.
Tokens per second per request.
Aggregate tokens per second.
Requests per second.
Queue time.
Prefill time.
Decode time.
KV cache memory.
Accelerator utilization.
CPU utilization.
Network utilization.
Cost per request.
Cost per successful task.
Quality or task-success score.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture
7  Metrics First: The Inference Control Plane

title: 7  Metrics First: The Inference Control Plane
text:
7.3 Measurement Rule
Every performance claim should name:

Model.
Hardware.
Runtime.
Precision or quantization.
Prompt length distribution.
Output length distribution.
Batch and concurrency settings.
Dataset or traffic source.
Percentile latency, not only averages.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture
7  Metrics First: The Inference Control Plane

title: 7  Metrics First: The Inference Control Plane
text:
7.4 Artifact
The reader creates a benchmark card that can be reused throughout the book.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture
7  Metrics First: The Inference Control Plane

title: 8  AI Hardware Taxonomy
text:
8.1 Chapter Job
Inference hardware is not one category. GPUs, CPUs, AI ASICs, LPUs, wafer-scale systems, and dataflow machines expose different tradeoffs.
Give the reader a hardware map before going vendor by vendor.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 8  AI Hardware Taxonomy
text:
8.2 Hardware Families

General-purpose CPUs.
GPUs.
Integrated CPU-GPU systems.
AI ASICs.
Dataflow accelerators.
Wafer-scale systems.
LPU-style deterministic token systems.
Edge accelerators.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 8  AI Hardware Taxonomy
text:
8.3 Comparison Axes

Memory capacity.
Memory bandwidth.
Compute throughput.
Interconnect.
Precision support.
Software maturity.
Compiler model.
Serving framework support.
Operational availability.
Workload fit.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 8  AI Hardware Taxonomy
text:
8.4 Inference Lens
Hardware should be evaluated by workload phase:

Prefill-heavy traffic.
Decode-heavy traffic.
Long-context traffic.
MoE routing and expert load balance.
Batch/offline inference.
Real-time interactive streaming.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 9  CPU-GPU Node Architecture
text:
9.1 Chapter Job
An inference node is a system of CPUs, memory, accelerators, NICs, storage, and software queues. The GPU is the center of gravity, but it is not the whole machine.
Explain how a single node moves work from process to CPU to GPU and back.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 9  CPU-GPU Node Architecture
text:
9.2 Node Components

CPU sockets.
NUMA domains.
System memory.
PCIe topology.
GPUs or accelerators.
GPU memory.
NICs.
Local NVMe.
Drivers and runtime libraries.
Container and orchestration layer.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 9  CPU-GPU Node Architecture
text:
9.3 Critical Paths





flowchart LR
  A[HTTP request] --> B[Runtime process]
  B --> C[Tokenizer and scheduler on CPU]
  C --> D[Host memory]
  D --> E[PCIe or local fabric]
  E --> F[Accelerator memory]
  F --> G[Kernels]
  G --> H[Generated tokens]
  H --> C
  C --> I[Streamed response]
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 9  CPU-GPU Node Architecture
text:
9.4 Failure Modes

CPU-side bottlenecks hidden by low GPU utilization.
NUMA mismatch between process, NIC, and GPU.
PCIe topology limiting multi-GPU communication.
Tokenization or post-processing blocking streaming.
Storage path slowing model load and warmup.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 10  Configurable Hardware Surfaces
text:
10.1 Chapter Job
Inference engineers rarely redesign silicon, but they do configure the system that exposes the silicon.
List the hardware and system-level controls an inference engineer can actually change.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 10  Configurable Hardware Surfaces
text:
10.2 Configuration Surfaces

GPU clocks and power limits.
MIG or partitioning modes where available.
NUMA pinning.
CPU affinity.
Huge pages and memory behavior.
PCIe and NIC placement.
Driver, CUDA, ROCm, oneAPI, or vendor runtime versions.
Container image and kernel module compatibility.
Precision and quantization choices.
Tensor parallel and pipeline parallel layout.
Batch size and scheduler knobs.
KV cache allocation.
Model placement and warmup policy.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 10  Configurable Hardware Surfaces
text:
10.3 Discipline
Every configuration change needs a before/after benchmark and a rollback path. The book treats configuration as an experimental system, not as a bag of tuning tips.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 11  NVIDIA Generations and Patterns
text:
11.1 Chapter Job
NVIDIA hardware is the default reference point for much of the inference ecosystem, so the book needs a generation-aware map.
Compare NVIDIA generations by the patterns that matter for inference rather than memorizing product names.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 11  NVIDIA Generations and Patterns
text:
11.2 Topics

CUDA programming model as the dominant software contract.
Tensor cores and precision evolution.
HBM capacity and bandwidth.
NVLink and node-scale fabrics.
MIG and partitioning concepts.
Transformer Engine-style precision trends.
Blackwell-generation themes to source-verify before publication.
How vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, Triton, and CUDA kernels sit on this stack.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 11  NVIDIA Generations and Patterns
text:
11.3 Pattern Questions

What changed in memory capacity?
What changed in memory bandwidth?
What changed in interconnect?
What changed in supported precision?
What changed in software path maturity?
What new workload class became practical?
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 11  NVIDIA Generations and Patterns
text:
11.4 Artifact
Create a generation comparison table with source links and inference-relevant columns only.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 12  Intel Architectures in Detail
text:
12.1 Chapter Job
Intel matters in inference both as the CPU host architecture and as an accelerator ecosystem. This chapter receives deeper treatment than a simple vendor survey.
Explain Intel-relevant inference architecture from CPU features to accelerator placement, software stack, and deployment tradeoffs.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 12  Intel Architectures in Detail
text:
12.2 CPU-Side Topics

Xeon server CPU role in inference nodes.
NUMA topology and memory channels.
PCIe lanes and accelerator attachment.
AMX and CPU inference acceleration.
Host-side tokenization, scheduling, networking, and preprocessing.
CPU-only inference workloads and when they are reasonable.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 12  Intel Architectures in Detail
text:
12.3 Accelerator-Side Topics

Gaudi-class accelerator positioning.
HBM, on-package memory, and network fabric concepts.
Collective communication and scale-out implications.
Software stack and framework integration.
Precision support and compiler/runtime path.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 12  Intel Architectures in Detail
text:
12.4 What to Compare Against

NVIDIA GPU-based serving.
AMD GPU-based serving.
CPU-only serving.
Specialized dataflow or LPU-style systems.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 12  Intel Architectures in Detail
text:
12.5 Publication Note
This chapter should be written from official Intel architecture sources, validated benchmark reports, and hands-on experiments. Treat secondary claims as leads, not facts.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 13  AMD Architecture Patterns
text:
13.1 Chapter Job
AMD should be treated as a serious inference option with its own hardware and software tradeoffs, not as a footnote.
Map AMD CPU and GPU architecture patterns that affect inference deployment.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 13  AMD Architecture Patterns
text:
13.2 Topics

EPYC host CPU role in dense inference nodes.
NUMA, memory channels, PCIe lanes, and host-side throughput.
Instinct GPU family as an accelerator option.
HBM capacity and bandwidth patterns.
ROCm software stack maturity and compatibility questions.
Framework support and operational gaps to test directly.
Multi-GPU and multi-node communication considerations.
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 13  AMD Architecture Patterns
text:
13.3 Comparison Questions

Which workloads are attractive on AMD hardware?
Which software path is mature enough for production?
What runtime and kernel support is available?
What is the debugging story?
How does cost/performance compare after operational effort is counted?
crumbs: Part II: Silicon, Nodes, and Hardware Architecture

title: 14  Transformer Inference Mechanics
text:
14.1 Chapter Job
The hardware chapters explain where the work runs. This chapter explains what the work is.
Explain transformer inference in the terms an infrastructure engineer needs: prefill, decode, memory movement, KV cache, and batching.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference

title: 14  Transformer Inference Mechanics
text:
14.2 Core Mechanics

Tokenization.
Embedding lookup.
Transformer block execution.
Attention.
MLP.
Normalization.
Logits and sampling.
Prefill phase.
Decode phase.
Streaming response.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference

title: 14  Transformer Inference Mechanics
text:
14.3 Why Prefill and Decode Differ
Prefill processes a prompt in parallel. Decode generates tokens one step at a time. Their bottlenecks, batching behavior, and memory pressure differ.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference

title: 14  Transformer Inference Mechanics
text:
14.4 Artifact
The reader creates a prefill/decode timeline for one model serving request.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference

title: 15  Attention, KV Cache, and Long Context
text:
15.1 Chapter Job
Attention is where model architecture becomes an inference systems problem.
Connect attention mechanisms to memory footprint, latency, batching, and long context behavior.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference
15  Attention, KV Cache, and Long Context

title: 15  Attention, KV Cache, and Long Context
text:
15.2 Topics

Multi-head attention.
Grouped-query attention.
Multi-query attention.
KV cache allocation.
KV cache fragmentation.
Prefix caching.
Sliding window and long-context strategies.
RAG versus long-context tradeoffs.
Why context length changes throughput and cost.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference
15  Attention, KV Cache, and Long Context

title: 15  Attention, KV Cache, and Long Context
text:
15.3 Practical Questions

How much KV memory does this workload need?
Does the serving runtime page or compact KV cache efficiently?
Does prefix reuse matter for this traffic?
Is long context solving the product problem or hiding retrieval design debt?
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference
15  Attention, KV Cache, and Long Context

title: 16  MoE, State Space, and Specialized Models
text:
16.1 Chapter Job
Not every model stresses the inference stack in the same way.
Explain why model architecture changes serving behavior.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference
16  MoE, State Space, and Specialized Models

title: 16  MoE, State Space, and Specialized Models
text:
16.2 MoE Topics

Expert routing.
Active parameters versus total parameters.
Load balance.
Expert parallelism.
Network pressure.
Batch composition.
Tail latency risks.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference
16  MoE, State Space, and Specialized Models

title: 16  MoE, State Space, and Specialized Models
text:
16.3 State-Space and Alternative Architectures

Why non-attention architectures are attractive for long sequences.
What changes in memory and compute profile.
What serving engines need to support.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference
16  MoE, State Space, and Specialized Models

title: 16  MoE, State Space, and Specialized Models
text:
16.4 Specialized Models

Embedding models.
Rerankers.
Vision-language models.
Speech models.
Small language models as probes and production workhorses.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference
16  MoE, State Space, and Specialized Models

title: 17  RL and Policy Inference
text:
17.1 Chapter Job
RL-related inference workloads often look different from chatbot serving.
Explain advanced inference patterns that appear in RL, alignment, simulation, search, and policy evaluation.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference

title: 17  RL and Policy Inference
text:
17.2 Workload Patterns

Many short rollouts.
Batched policy evaluation.
Tree search or verifier-guided generation.
Reward model scoring.
Rejection sampling.
Self-play or simulation loops.
Online policy serving.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference

title: 17  RL and Policy Inference
text:
17.3 Systems Consequences

Small latency differences multiply across rollouts.
Batch shape can be highly irregular.
CPU-side orchestration can dominate accelerator time.
Caching and reuse may be possible across search branches.
Quality metrics need task-level outcomes, not only token-level speed.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference

title: 17  RL and Policy Inference
text:
17.4 Bridge
The next section moves from model mechanics to the kernels and compiler paths that make these workloads fast enough to matter.
crumbs: Part III: Model Mechanics That Matter for Inference

title: 18  Kernels and Compiler Path
text:
18.1 Chapter Job
Kernel engineering matters because inference is often limited by memory movement and launch overhead, not just arithmetic throughput.
Give the reader enough kernel and compiler literacy to understand runtime choices without becoming a kernel specialist.
crumbs: Part IV: Kernels, Compilers, and Runtime Primitives

title: 18  Kernels and Compiler Path
text:
18.2 Topics

What a kernel is.
Kernel launch overhead.
Memory coalescing.
Tiling.
Fused operations.
Attention kernels.
GEMM kernels.
Sampling kernels.
Triton.
CUTLASS-style libraries.
torch.compile and graph capture.
TensorRT-LLM-style optimized paths.
crumbs: Part IV: Kernels, Compilers, and Runtime Primitives

title: 18  Kernels and Compiler Path
text:
18.3 Depth Boundary
This chapter should teach how to reason about kernels, inspect whether optimized kernels are being used, and understand why a runtime gets faster. It does not need to teach full CUDA kernel authorship.
crumbs: Part IV: Kernels, Compilers, and Runtime Primitives

title: 19  Memory Bandwidth, Quantization, and Numerics
text:
19.1 Chapter Job
Many inference optimizations are memory optimizations wearing a model name.
Explain how precision and quantization change memory, speed, quality, and hardware compatibility.
crumbs: Part IV: Kernels, Compilers, and Runtime Primitives
19  Memory Bandwidth, Quantization, and Numerics

title: 19  Memory Bandwidth, Quantization, and Numerics
text:
19.2 Topics

BF16, FP16, FP8, INT8, INT4, and mixed precision.
Weight-only quantization.
Activation quantization.
KV cache quantization.
Calibration.
Quality evaluation.
Bandwidth versus compute bottlenecks.
Quantization support across runtimes and hardware.
crumbs: Part IV: Kernels, Compilers, and Runtime Primitives
19  Memory Bandwidth, Quantization, and Numerics

title: 19  Memory Bandwidth, Quantization, and Numerics
text:
19.3 Discipline
No quantization claim is complete without:

Model.
Dataset or task.
Hardware.
Runtime.
Precision format.
Latency and throughput result.
Quality regression result.
crumbs: Part IV: Kernels, Compilers, and Runtime Primitives
19  Memory Bandwidth, Quantization, and Numerics

title: 20  Serving Engines and Runtime Map
text:
20.1 Chapter Job
Serving engines are where model mechanics meet scheduler policy.
Map the runtime ecosystem and explain why the book uses a framework-first path.
crumbs: 20  Serving Engines and Runtime Map

title: 20  Serving Engines and Runtime Map
text:
20.2 Runtime Families

vLLM.
SGLang.
TensorRT-LLM.
Triton Inference Server.
Text Generation Inference.
llama.cpp and local runtimes.
Managed provider runtimes.

title: 20  Serving Engines and Runtime Map
text:
20.3 Comparison Axes

Scheduler design.
KV cache management.
Continuous batching.
Prefix caching.
Structured output and tool support.
Quantization support.
Tensor and pipeline parallel support.
Observability hooks.
Deployment maturity.
crumbs: 20  Serving Engines and Runtime Map

title: 20  Serving Engines and Runtime Map
text:
20.4 Book Default
The primary implementation path is vLLM because it is practical, visible, widely used, and exposes the right concepts for learning.
crumbs: 20  Serving Engines and Runtime Map

title: 21  vLLM From First Principles
text:
21.1 Chapter Job
vLLM is the book’s main hands-on serving engine.
Teach vLLM as an inference systems artifact: scheduler, memory manager, API server, and performance surface.

title: 21  vLLM From First Principles
text:
21.2 Topics

OpenAI-compatible server.
Offline batch inference.
Continuous batching.
PagedAttention.
KV cache allocation.
Prefix caching.
Tensor parallelism.
Quantization support.
Sampling configuration.
Streaming.
Metrics endpoint.
Common OOM and capacity failures.

title: 21  vLLM From First Principles
text:
21.3 Lab Path
The reader should implement:

Serve a small model.
Run a streaming probe.
Measure TTFT and total latency.
Change concurrency and output length.
Enable prefix caching where applicable.
Capture metrics and compare results.

title: 22  Runpod.ai Practical Deployment
text:
22.1 Chapter Job
Runpod is the book’s default on-demand GPU lab environment.
Give the reader a practical path to rent GPU capacity, launch a pod, run vLLM, and collect benchmark results.

title: 22  Runpod.ai Practical Deployment
text:
22.2 Workflow

Choose a GPU based on model size and memory needs.
Start a pod with a suitable container image.
Attach persistent storage if needed.
Install or use a prebuilt vLLM environment.
Start the vLLM OpenAI-compatible server.
Expose the endpoint safely.
Run a benchmark from a client.
Save metrics and configuration.
Stop resources when done.

title: 22  Runpod.ai Practical Deployment
text:
22.3 Operational Warnings

Track cost while experimenting.
Avoid exposing unauthenticated endpoints.
Record exact GPU, image, driver, CUDA, Python, and vLLM versions.
Treat ephemeral pods as disposable unless storage is explicitly configured.

title: 22  Runpod.ai Practical Deployment
text:
22.4 Artifact
A reproducible Runpod experiment card:

Pod type.
GPU.
Model.
Runtime command.
Benchmark command.
Results table.
Failure notes.

title: 23  Benchmarking Harness
text:
23.1 Chapter Job
Benchmarks are the book’s source of truth.
Define a harness that can compare runtimes, hardware, models, and settings without changing the question each time.

title: 23  Benchmarking Harness
text:
23.2 Harness Components

Prompt dataset.
Output length control.
Concurrency driver.
Streaming probe.
Metrics collector.
Result summarizer.
Environment snapshot.
Cost calculator.
Plotting script.

title: 23  Benchmarking Harness
text:
23.3 Benchmark Types

Single request trace.
Concurrency sweep.
Prompt-length sweep.
Output-length sweep.
Quantization comparison.
Prefix-cache experiment.
Multi-tenant load test.
Failure and retry drill.

title: 23  Benchmarking Harness
text:
23.4 Source Link
This chapter should promote material from the local inference-journal benchmarking and latency/SLO labs.

title: 24  Speculative Decoding
text:
24.1 Chapter Job
Speculative decoding is a good example of an advanced method that only makes sense when model, runtime, and hardware behavior are understood together.
Explain draft/target decoding, when it helps, and how to measure it.

title: 24  Speculative Decoding
text:
24.2 Topics

Draft model.
Target model.
Acceptance rate.
Verification cost.
Latency speedup.
Quality preservation.
Workload sensitivity.
Runtime support.

title: 24  Speculative Decoding
text:
24.3 Measurement Questions

What is the acceptance rate?
Does TTFT improve, total latency improve, or both?
Does speedup hold at concurrency?
Does the draft model consume scarce hardware capacity?
Does implementation complexity justify the win?

title: 25  Disaggregated Inference Architectures
text:
25.1 Chapter Job
Disaggregation separates parts of the inference path so each can scale and optimize differently.
Explain why systems separate prefill, decode, memory, routing, and serving responsibilities.

title: 25  Disaggregated Inference Architectures
text:
25.2 Patterns

Prefill/decode disaggregation.
KV cache movement or reuse.
Router and scheduler separation.
Multi-tier serving.
CPU offload.
Remote memory or cache services.
Specialized hardware per phase.

title: 25  Disaggregated Inference Architectures
text:
25.3 Tradeoffs

Higher utilization versus higher coordination complexity.
Better specialization versus more network dependence.
Lower cost at scale versus harder debugging.
Cleaner resource pools versus new failure modes.

title: 25  Disaggregated Inference Architectures
text:
25.4 Placement
This chapter belongs before the specialized system survey because it gives the reader a general vocabulary for architectures from SambaNova, Cerebras, Groq, and related systems.

title: 26  Specialized Inference Systems: SambaNova, Cerebras, Groq
text:
26.1 Chapter Job
Specialized systems change the architecture conversation. They are not just faster GPUs; they often expose a different execution model.
Compare specialized inference architectures by workload fit, software contract, scaling model, and operational tradeoff.
crumbs: 26  Specialized Inference Systems: SambaNova, Cerebras, Groq

title: 26  Specialized Inference Systems: SambaNova, Cerebras, Groq
text:
26.2 Systems to Cover

SambaNova-style dataflow architecture.
Cerebras-style wafer-scale architecture.
Groq-style deterministic token pipeline and LPU framing.
crumbs: 26  Specialized Inference Systems: SambaNova, Cerebras, Groq

title: 26  Specialized Inference Systems: SambaNova, Cerebras, Groq
text:
26.3 Comparison Axes

Programming model.
Compiler path.
Memory model.
Network or scale-out model.
Latency profile.
Throughput profile.
Model compatibility.
Serving interface.
Deployment model.
Operational maturity.
crumbs: 26  Specialized Inference Systems: SambaNova, Cerebras, Groq

title: 26  Specialized Inference Systems: SambaNova, Cerebras, Groq
text:
26.4 Rule
This chapter must separate vendor claims from measured behavior. Each system gets a source-verified architecture summary and a workload-fit analysis.
crumbs: 26  Specialized Inference Systems: SambaNova, Cerebras, Groq

title: 27  Multi-GPU and Multi-Node Scaling
text:
27.1 Chapter Job
Scaling inference is not just adding more GPUs. The communication pattern has to match the model and runtime.
Teach the scaling strategies that show up in real inference deployments.

title: 27  Multi-GPU and Multi-Node Scaling
text:
27.2 Topics

Data parallel serving.
Tensor parallelism.
Pipeline parallelism.
Expert parallelism.
Sequence parallel and long-context variants.
NCCL and collectives.
Topology-aware placement.
Multi-node routing.
Failure domains.

title: 27  Multi-GPU and Multi-Node Scaling
text:
27.3 Diagnostic Questions

Is the workload compute-bound, memory-bound, network-bound, or scheduler-bound?
Is the model too large for one accelerator?
Does parallelism improve latency, throughput, or only capacity?
Which link is in the critical path?
How does tail latency change at scale?

title: 28  Multitenancy and QoS
text:
28.1 Chapter Job
Inference platforms become hard when many users, models, and workloads share the same capacity.
Explain fairness, isolation, scheduling, and degradation in multi-tenant inference systems.

title: 28  Multitenancy and QoS
text:
28.2 Topics

Tenant-aware queues.
Priority classes.
Rate limits.
Admission control.
Deadline-aware scheduling.
Per-tenant cost attribution.
Noisy-neighbor failures.
Model pool isolation.
Shared versus dedicated capacity.
Graceful degradation.

title: 28  Multitenancy and QoS
text:
28.3 Artifact
Design a QoS policy for three tenants:

Interactive product traffic.
Internal batch jobs.
Evaluation workloads.

title: 29  Evals and Regression Testing
text:
29.1 Chapter Job
Inference changes can be technically faster and product-worse. Evals prevent that from becoming normal.
Define the release gates for prompt, model, runtime, quantization, and hardware changes.

title: 29  Evals and Regression Testing
text:
29.2 Eval Layers

Unit tests for prompt assembly.
Schema validity tests.
Golden examples.
Task-specific graders.
Human review.
Safety and policy tests.
Latency and cost regression tests.
Shadow traffic.

title: 29  Evals and Regression Testing
text:
29.3 Release Principle
Every inference optimization needs two results:

Did it improve the target system metric?
Did it preserve or improve task quality?

title: 30  Observability, Reliability, and Security
text:
30.1 Chapter Job
Production inference needs one integrated operating model. Observability, reliability, and security cannot be bolted on after the benchmark.
Define the production control surface for inference systems.

title: 30  Observability, Reliability, and Security
text:
30.2 Observability

Request ID.
Model and runtime version.
Prompt template version.
Token counts.
Prefill and decode timing.
Queue time.
Retry count.
Output validity.
User or tenant class.

title: 30  Observability, Reliability, and Security
text:
30.3 Reliability

Timeout policy.
Retry policy.
Circuit breakers.
Fallback models.
Queue limits.
Degradation modes.
Capacity reservations.
Incident drills.

title: 30  Observability, Reliability, and Security
text:
30.4 Security

Input validation.
Prompt injection controls.
Tool permissions.
Tenant isolation.
Log redaction.
Secret handling.
Cache boundaries.
Vendor and region policy.

title: 31  Cost and Capacity Planning
text:
31.1 Chapter Job
Cost is a systems metric. It depends on model, workload, hardware, scheduler, utilization, and failure behavior.
Give the reader a capacity and unit-economics method for inference systems.

title: 31  Cost and Capacity Planning
text:
31.2 Inputs

Request rate.
Prompt length distribution.
Output length distribution.
Model mix.
Concurrency.
Target SLO.
Accelerator cost.
Utilization.
Retry rate.
Cache hit rate.
Quality threshold.

title: 31  Cost and Capacity Planning
text:
31.3 Outputs

Cost per request.
Cost per successful task.
Required accelerators.
Burst headroom.
Failure reserve.
Per-tenant margin or budget.

title: 31  Cost and Capacity Planning
text:
31.4 Artifact
A capacity model that converts product traffic into token load, hardware need, and cost.

title: 32  Case Studies and Capstones
text:
32.1 Chapter Job
The book should end with built systems, not just explanations.
Turn the book into public artifacts that prove engineering judgment.

title: 32  Case Studies and Capstones
text:
32.2 Case Studies

Interactive chat assistant with streaming SLOs.
Long-context analyst workflow.
RAG versus long-context comparison.
Document extraction with structured output.
Multi-tenant vLLM endpoint.
Speculative decoding experiment.
Runpod benchmark report.
Specialized architecture comparison.
Cost and capacity planning memo.

title: 32  Case Studies and Capstones
text:
32.3 Capstone Format
Each capstone should include:

Problem statement.
Inference path.
Hardware and runtime choice.
Benchmark setup.
Results.
Quality evaluation.
Cost model.
Failure modes.
What changed after measurement.

title: Appendix A — Source Map

A.3 GitHub Repo Note
The requested GitHub repository ozgurgulerx/inference-engineering did not resolve through the GitHub CLI during setup. If it is private, renamed, or under a different owner, update this appendix after adding the remote or cloning it locally.

title: Appendix B — Structure Review
text:
B.1 Coherence Check
This appendix records the editorial logic of the book so the outline can be maintained as it grows.
The book now follows a physical-to-product progression:
This order prevents the reader from meeting advanced runtime choices before they understand the physical and architectural constraints those choices are trying to manage.

title: Appendix B — Structure Review
text:
Data center constraints.
Hardware and node architecture.
Model mechanics.
Kernels and runtimes.
Framework-first implementation.
Advanced architectures.
Production engineering.

title: Appendix B — Structure Review
text:
B.2 Narrative Progression
The narrative moves from “where do tokens physically run?” to “how do we make that path measurable, fast, reliable, and cost-effective?”
Each part should answer one question:

Data center: what physical envelope are we operating inside?
Hardware: what can the node and accelerator actually do?
Models: what work does the model impose on the system?
Kernels: what makes execution efficient?
Frameworks: how do we implement the concepts quickly?
Advanced systems: what changes at scale or with specialized architectures?
Production: how do we operate and improve the system safely?

title: Appendix B — Structure Review
text:
B.3 Balance Check
The current outline is intentionally broad but not evenly deep.
Deep chapters:

Metrics.
CPU-GPU node architecture.
Intel architectures.
Attention and KV cache.
vLLM.
Benchmarking.
Speculative decoding.
Disaggregated inference.
Production observability and capacity planning.

Survey chapters:

AMD architecture patterns until experiments are available.
Specialized systems until architecture claims are source-verified.
Kernel engineering beyond runtime-level literacy.
Modalities outside core LLM inference.

title: Appendix B — Structure Review
text:
B.4 Readability Rule
Every chapter should contain:

A concrete reader outcome.
A short system diagram, table, or decision matrix.
A practical artifact.
A “what to measure” section.
A “what can go wrong” section.

title: Appendix B — Structure Review
text:
B.5 Missing Work

Source-verify vendor architecture claims.
Add citations and reference links.
Convert local journal experiments into polished labs.
Add runnable Runpod and vLLM scripts.
Add benchmark result templates.
Decide whether to merge or archive the older unused chapter stubs.

title: Appendix C — Glossary
text:
Batching

Combining multiple inference requests into one model-server operation.

Continuous batching

A serving-engine scheduling technique that admits and completes requests dynamically rather than waiting for a fixed batch to finish.

Context assembly

Building the information sent to the model from user input, system state, retrieval, tools, and templates.

Decode

The token-by-token generation phase after the prompt has been processed.

Disaggregated inference

An architecture that separates phases or responsibilities such as prefill, decode, routing, cache, and memory so they can scale independently.

Eval

A repeatable measurement of model or system behavior against examples, criteria, or human judgment.

Expert parallelism

A scaling strategy used by mixture-of-experts models where experts are placed across devices or nodes.

Inference path

The runtime sequence from user request to model output to product response.

Inter-token latency

The time between streamed output tokens during decode.

KV cache

Cached key/value tensors used by transformer decoders to avoid recomputing attention over previous tokens.

MoE

Mixture of experts, a model architecture where a router activates a subset of expert networks for a given token or request.

Prefill

The phase that processes the input prompt before token-by-token generation.

Prompt contract

The versioned agreement describing input shape, instructions, tool access, output schema, and expected behavior.

QoS

Quality of service; the policy layer that manages priority, fairness, admission, and degradation across tenants or workloads.

Speculative decoding

A decoding method where a draft model proposes tokens and a target model verifies them to reduce generation latency when acceptance is high.

Time to first token

Time from request start to the first streamed output token.

Time to last token

Time from request start to the completed model output.

title: Appendix D — Checklists
text: D.1 Data Center Design Review

title: Appendix D — Checklists
text:
Workload class is defined.
Rack power envelope is documented.
Cooling method and density target are documented.
Network topology is mapped.
Storage and model-load path are documented.
Failure domains are named.
Benchmark evidence exists for the proposed deployment shape.

title: Appendix D — Checklists
text:
D.2 Hardware Selection

Model size and context length are known.
Precision and quantization targets are known.
Prefill and decode workload mix is estimated.
HBM capacity and bandwidth needs are estimated.
CPU, memory, PCIe, NIC, and accelerator topology are reviewed.
Runtime support is verified.
Cost and availability are included in the decision.

title: Appendix D — Checklists
text:
D.3 New Inference Feature

Product owner is named.
Inference path is drawn.
Prompt contract is versioned.
Output schema is validated.
Latency budget is defined.
Cost owner is defined.
Eval set exists.
Logs and traces include request IDs.
Sensitive data handling is documented.
Fallback behavior is tested.

title: Appendix D — Checklists
text:
D.4 Model or Prompt Change

Change reason is documented.
Eval run is attached to the PR.
Regression failures are reviewed.
Rollout percentage is defined.
Dashboard is linked.
Rollback path is known.

title: Appendix D — Checklists
text:
D.5 Benchmark Report

Model, runtime, hardware, and versions are recorded.
Prompt and output distributions are described.
Concurrency and batch settings are recorded.
TTFT, inter-token latency, total latency, and throughput are included.
Quality or task-success check is included.
Cost estimate is included.
Raw results are saved.

title: Appendix D — Checklists
text:
D.6 Incident Review

User impact is stated in product terms.
Failed boundary is identified.
Missing signal is named.
Preventive test or alert is added.
Owner and due date are assigned.
